title: Äskhult Village

text: In the forests of Halland lies Äskhult village, like a frozen snapshot of the past. But behind the weathered timber walls and the untouched cattle paths hides more than just beautiful aesthetics – here lies a wealth of human destinies, legal documents, and an agricultural evolution spanning centuries.

Here, we have gathered research on the four farms: Bengts, Derras, Jönsas, and Göttas. Dive into historical events, fascinating myths, and local legends. You can also follow along with our investigation.

The farms in Äskhult belonged to Förlanda parish in the census records, but it is commonly said that the village is located in Fjärås. Äskhult balances precisely on the border between these two parishes.
The lands around Äskhult have been cultivated since the Iron Age. There are ancient remains nearby, indicating that the site has been inhabited and farmed for over a thousand years.

For us at Historiska Röster, this has been one of the most challenging projects to date regarding the research portion. In the household examination records, we were met with buildings without names, nearly identical personal names, and faded handwriting that sometimes required a magnifying glass, patience, and a fair share of detective work to decipher.

The farm consists of a residential house and a barn and is named after the Member of Parliament Bengt Pehrsson (b. 1759 16/3 - d. 1837). The farm was struck by an extensive fire in 1742. The barn dates from 1610 and is likely the oldest building in the village, while the residential house is from 1850 and was extended in 1914 to enlarge the kitchen. The last owners of the house were August and Hanna Göök, who owned the farm privately before it was sold to the municipality in 1958.

The last owner in the household records from 1900 shows that Petter Persson (b. 1833 16/2) took over the farm in 1869. He married Johanna Lena Johansdotter (b. 1839 7/7 - d. 1888 22/12) in 1866, and they had children: Sofia Albertina (b. 1867 8/4), Anna Mathilda (b. 1870 27/1), Anna Lena (b. 1872 5/9 - d. 1875 31/1), Anna Lena (b. 1875 30/7), and Anders Peter (b. 1880 16/5). During certain periods, the foster daughter Adelina Albertina Bengtsdotter (b. 1860 20/10) from Wallda lived with the family, as well as the illegitimate child Alfred Julius (b. 1894 25/5).

Petter's brother, named Anders Persson (b. 1830 23/9 - d. 1898 23/10), and his wife Johanna Månsdotter (b. 1838 16/5) lived in a cottage on the farm with their children Anders Petter (b. 1876 18/2), Johan Aron (b. 1877 18/2), Johan Martin (b. 1878 9/5), and Klara Albertina (b. 1882 3/4).

Before Petter, the farm was owned by Bengt Pehrsson (b. 1759 16/3). It is he who gave the farm its name. He moved in in 1785 and married Gunnilla Andersdotter (b. 1756 19/11). They had children: Maria (b. 1785 11/12), Ingrid (b. 1785 22/9), Anders (b. 1787 16/10), Pehr (b. 1791 28/11), Brita (b. 1797 19/8), Börge (b. 1794 23/7 - d. 1796 25/3), and Börge (b. 1801 1/8). Gunnilla died between 1804-1814. Bengt's parents Per Börgesson (b. 1732 21/8) and Marit (b. 1726 25/12 - d. 1800 5/6) moved in in 1795 and lived there until their deaths. Bengt became a lay assessor and took over after the three Jöns-sons from Jönsas farm.
The household records do not stretch further back than Bengt Pehrsson's ownership in 1789.

The last head of the household we can see in the records was Lars Andersson (b. 1831 1/10 - d. 1878 28/2), who married Gunnor Andersdotter (b. 1842 20/11) in 1870. They had children: Anders Benjamin (b. 1870 16/12), Aron (b. 1873 4/5), Johan Albin (b. 1875 1/8), and Anna Lena (b. 1878 3/10). His brother Johannes Andersson (b. 1840 10/9) and one Mariana (b. 1842 9/11) lived with the family for a period. The son Anders Benjamin emigrated to America in 1891, and the daughter Anna Lena moved to Wallda in 1896 but returned in 1897.

Johannes Andersson (b. 1855 19/7 - d. 1892 30/8) also lived in the same household with his wife Adelina Albertina Bengtsdotter (b. 1860 20/10). They married in 1881 and moved to the house, where they had daughters Anna Augustina (b. 1881 27/9) and Ida Amanda (b. 1887 7/8). The illegitimate child Jenny Alfrida (b. 1897 4/9) also lived with the family. It appears the family moved to America.

Before Lars, the house was owned by Anders Börjesson (b. 1799 30/5 - d. 1863 17/8) and his wife Anna Larsdotter (b. 1798 1/4 - d. 1861 18/5). They had children: Börje (b. 1830 23/7 - d. 1863 25/1), Lars (b. 1831 1/10), Johannes (b. 1840 10/9), Anna Britta (b. 1844 16/7), and Mariana (b. 1842 9/11).

Before Anders, the farm was owned by Bengt Pehrsson's son Pehr Bengtsson (b. 1791 28/11 - d. 1836 24/8) and his wife Anna Britta Andersdotter (b. 1796 16/2 - d. 1866 18/2). They had children: Johannes (b. 1823 15/1), Anders (b. 1825 11/2), Bengt (b. 1827 23/12), twins Anders & Magnus (b. 1830 28/4), and Gunnor (b. 1821 16/2). When Pehr died in 1836, Anna Britta and the remaining children moved into Bengts Farm 1, and Lars took over. Börje Bengtsson (b. 1801 1/8 - d. 1852 25/2) also lived with Anna and the children.
Prior to this, specifically before 1814, this was part of Bengts Farm.

The name Derras comes from the word 'grannens' (the neighbor's) and the farm has always carried this name. The residential house was likely originally a 'högloftsstuga' (high-loft cottage), and the farm also includes a stable with a pigsty and a barn. The farm seems to have been constructed in its current form between 1824–1826.

The last person to live in the village almost until his death was Gottfrid Pettersson (b. 1897 18/5 – d. 1964). He died an unmarried bachelor and was the last resident in the entire village. Gottfrid became a well-known 'original' in the area, famous for his stories as a poacher. One famous episode involves him hunting moose with homemade dum-dum bullets; after a dramatic chase, he was forced to finish the task with a knife and butcher the moose on the spot to secretly carry the meat back to his own land.

Gottfrid's father is the last person seen owning the farm in the records. His name was Peter Olsson (b. 1829 1/10), and he married Johanna Lena Ljunggren (b. 1862 23/8) in 1894. They had children: Karl Algot (b. 1894 4/12 - d. 1918) and Gottfrid (b. 1897 18/5). The illegitimate child Matilda Oktavia (b. 1884 4/7) also lived with the family starting from 1897. Since Gottfrid remained unmarried, Karl Algot died of the Spanish flu in America at age 24, and half-sister Matilda Oktavia died at age 23, the farm did not pass down through the family line.

Before Peter, the farm was owned by Johannes Andersson (b. 1848 8/10), who married Anna Brita Andersdotter (b. 1850 27/3) in 1876. Their flock of children was large: Johan August (b. 1878 7/5), Anders Edvin (b. 1880 26/2 – d. 1880 5/12), Anders Peter (b. 1881 16/9 – d. 1881 21/11), Johan Edvin (b. 1882 - d. 1891 25/3), Anders Peter (b. 1885 31/1), Ida Augusta (b. 1888 1/1), and Olga Matilda (b. 1890 15/3).

Before Johannes, the farm was owned by his father Anders Johansson (b. 1820 7/7 – d. 1869 26/5) and mother Johanna Olofsdotter (b. 1825 1/2). They had children: Johannes (b. 1848 8/10), Olof (b. 1851 5/12), Brother Börje (b. 1858 8/7), Brita Lena (b. 1861 2/12), and Anders Peter (b. 1868 27/7 - d. 1869 22/2). Living with the family were also the father Johannes Svensson (b. 1794 2/5) and Christina Pehrsdotter (b. 1799 17/2 - d. 1869 1/2), as well as the daughter Anna Christina (b. 1839 26/1).

Before that, the farm was owned by Johannes Andersson (b. 1793 20/2 - d. 1859 24/4) and his wife Anna Britta Persdotter (b. 1805 1/10). They had a son, Andreas (b. 1841 16/9 - d. 1860 19/1), and daughters Anna Christina (b. 1833 4/11), Mariana (b. 1839 22/4), Johanna (b. 1844 30/7), and Anna Sara (b. 1847 24/10). Johannes' parents Jöns Andersson (b. 1768 24/1) and Anna Larsdotter (b. 1774), along with their daughter Börta (b. 1810 15/10), also lived on the farm.

Before Johannes, the farm was owned by Börje Olofsson (b. 1771 4/3 - d. 1836 4/2). He lived there with his wife Börtta Andersdotter (b. 1772) and their two sons Anders (b. 1799 30/5) and Olof (b. 1805 27/9). Börtta's mother Britta Börjesdotter (b. 1744 20/6) also lived with the family.

As far as we can see in the records, the farm was owned by an Anders Persson (b. 1730), who was married to Brita Börgesdotter (b. 1744 20/6). They had children: Gunnar (b. 1780), Brita (b. 1787 5/5), Per (b. 1783 13/12), and Börje Olsson (b. 1771).

The last owner visible in the records is Anders Persson (b. 1835 4/9), who married Mariana Johansdotter (b. 1839 22/4) in 1862. They had children: Johan Petter (b. 1862 16/8), Anna Britta (b. 1866 20/3), Aron (b. 1870 1/7), Johannes (b. 1870 1/7), and Anders (b. 1874 3/10). Both sons emigrated to America, but Johannes returned in 1897 to work on the farm. Mother-in-law Anna Britta Christiansdotter (b. 1805 1/10 - d. 1889 5/3) and brother Anders Pehrsson (b. 1830 23/9 - d. 1884 9/10) also lived with the family on the farm.

Before Anders' time, it was owned by Olof Andersson (b. 1851 5/12), son of Anders Johansson who owned the main building of Derras Farm. He gave his sons Olof and Johannes Andersson 1/8 of the farm each. Olof married Anna Sofia Andersdotter (b. 1855 3/9), and they had children Alma Josefina (b. 1876 13/8), Johanna Lena (b. 1880 2/9), and Anders (b. 1884 26/5).

The mother Johanna Olofsdotter (b. 1825 7/2 - d. 1887 12/6), grandfather Johannes Svensson (b. 1794 2/5 - d. 1879 16/2), brother Börje Andersson (b. 1858 8/7 - d. 1890 4/3), and sister Britta Lena Andersdotter (b. 1861 2/12) also lived either at Derras Farm or in the cottage during various periods.

Before Olof, the farm was owned by Johannes Svensson (b. 1794 2/5) and Christina Pehrsdotter (b. 1799 17/2 - d. 1869 1/2). They had a very large household with children: Anders (b. 1820 7/7), Pehr (b. 1823 29/1), Zacharias (b. 1826 5/2 - d. 1828), Zacharias (b. 1829 2/9 - d. 1829), Zacharias (b. 1833 5/2), Anna Christina (b. 1839 26/1), Andreas (b. 1842 16/2 - d. 1843 26/6), Johanna Lena (b. 1846 16/9 - d. 1847 28/3), Anna Britta (b. 1846 16/9 - d. 1847 12/2), and Johannes (b. 1848 8/10). Sister-in-law Anna Britta Pehrsdotter (b. 1804 12/11) also lived with the family.

Before Johannes, the farm was owned by Pehr Andersson (b. 1754 23/12), and the records do not stretch further back than that. He married Kjerstin Larsdotter (b. 1763 21/10), and they had children: Christina (b. 1785 8/12), Anders (b. 1790 27/11 - d. 1846 9/3), Lars (b. 1795 15/11), Christin (b. 1799 12/2), and Anna Britta (b. 1804 16/4). A Börta Persdotter (b. 1736 - d. 1799) and a Karin (b. 1751) also lived on the farm.

Today, the farm consists of a residential house and a barn. The current house was moved here from Timmerås between 1968–1970, after the original house tragically burned down in 1953. The house was placed there to restore the village's historical appearance. An interesting detail is that the house's original loom survived the fire as it was being stored in the barn at the time. To this day, fire damage can be seen on the part of the barn's facade facing the residential house. The barn was originally built during the 1700s and underwent a reconstruction during the 1870s.

The farm's early history was marked for more than a century by three generations named Jöns Jönsson. It was during this period, starting with the purchase of the farm in 1734, that the family's feared reputation began to grow in the region. Their strong influence and authoritarian presence left deep marks on the village's social life.
The last owner appearing in the household records was Magnus Svensson (b. 1824 25/12), who lived on the farm for a short period with his sister Anna Christina (b. 1836 17/8).

Before Magnus' time, the farm was owned by Lars Pehrsson (b. 1795 15/11) and his wife Britta Börgesdotter (b. 1789 30/4 - d. 1840 21/12). They had children: Johanna Beata (b. 1812), Anders (b. 1821 13/5), Magnus (b. 1823 25/12), Per (b. 1826 9/11), and Anders (b. 1830 21/1 - d. 1832).

Before Lars took over the farm, it was owned by Anders Jönsson, and before that by his father Jöns Andersson (b. 1768 27/1 - d. 1847 27/10). Jöns was first married to Maria Bengtsdotter (b. 1768 31/8 - d. 1805 16/4) and they had a son, Anders (b. 1801 10/1). After her death, he married Anna Pehrsdotter (b. 1774 - d. 1848 1/1) and they had children Bengt (b. 1805 22/2) and Börta (b. 1810 15/10). Parents Anders Jönsson (b. 1733 27/2) and Börta Månsdotter (b. 1739 24/3 - d. 1798) also lived on the farm.

Records also show that a cottage known as 'Backastugan' stood on the land until 1794/1795. Living there were Johannes Larsson (b. 1732) and his wife Karin Jönsdotter (b. Feb 15, 1733), along with their children: Lena Brita (b. Apr 24, 1762), Erik (b. Jan 1, 1769 – d. 1789), Kjerstin (b. Jan 12, 1778), and Maria (b. Nov 20, 1780).
The household examination records do not stretch further back than this.

The farm received its name in 1755 after Anders Persson from Gödatorp in Gällinge purchased it. Göttas is Äskhult's best-preserved farm and consists of a residential house, stable, barn, root cellar, carpentry workshop, and woodshed. The house and workshop were built in 1755, while the barn is as old as 1726. Following a fire in the 1700s, the farm was moved, which is one of the few major changes in the village layout. The farm was inhabited until 1945.

The farm's history holds several powerful life stories, from the tragic tale of the maid Kirstin in the 1740s who murdered her child, to the defiant maid Anna who later challenged the village hierarchy. During the 1800s, the farm gained special status in the village as it was inhabited by foresters. At a time when timber was a scarce resource, the task of protecting the Crown's forests was both important and respect-commanding.

The last owner in the household records is Magnus Persson (b. 1830 28/4), who moved in in 1859 with his wife Anna Lena Karlsdotter (b. 1836 17/2). The children were many: Britta Lena (b. 1860 13/1) who emigrated to Michigan in 1881, Johan (b. 1861 8/4 - d. 1863 5/7), Anna (b. 1864 4/7) who returned from the USA in 1894 with daughter Ester Alida Viktoria (b. 1899), Johan Petter (b. 1866 31/12) who moved to Michigan in 1886, Anders (b. 1871 27/5), Anna Martina (b. 1873 18/12), Johannes (b. 1876 7/4), and Clara Albertina (b. 1883 11/4 – d. 1883 25/4). Magnus' mother Anna Brita Andersdotter (b. 1802 30/2 – d. 1890 25/11) and sisters-in-law Anna Malena and Mariana Johansdotter also lived here periodically.

Before the Persson family, the farm was owned by the forester Johan Carl Wennerlund (b. 1799 6/8 - d. 1858 10/1) and his wife Anna Britta Andersdotter (b. 1802 30/3). Their children were Anders (b. 1849 27/12), Johannes (b. 1847 21/1 – d. 1847 21/10), Börta (b. 1834 3/11 – d. 1843 1/12), Mariana (b. 1837 13/7), Anna Lena (b. 1836 17/2), Anna Martina (b. 1843 23/3), and Börta (b. 1847 14/1 – d. 1847 7/4). The sister Anna Johanna (b. 1812 26/8) was also registered on the farm.

Johan Carl in turn took over from his father, Magnus Wennerlund (b. 1759 – d. 1829), who was also a forester. With his wife Börta Johansdotter (b. 1779 20/8), he had children Johan Carl (b. 1799 6/8) and Anna Johanna (b. 1812 26/8). During the 1800s, timber was scarce, and the forester's mission to protect the Crown's forests gave the family a special status in the village.

As far back as the church records reach, we see Erik Andersson (b. 1733 20/2 – d. 1793 21/4) as the owner. He lived there with his wife Anna Eriksdotter (b. 1735 23/10 - d. 1799), the maid Börta (b. 1779 20/5), and brother Börge Eriksson. There also seems to have been a cottage on the farm where the widow Bengta Andersdotter (b. 1718 5/12 - d. 1793) lived with her daughter Gunilla (b. 1746 12/12), as well as Carl Andersson (b. 1748 8/12) with his wife Kjestin Eriksdotter (b. 1753 22/4) and daughter Börta (b. 1786 15/11).

In the heart of the village lay the gathering place, often beneath a 'warden tree' (vårdträd). Here, village meetings were held where communal decisions were made on everything from sowing to harvesting. The reason Äskhult looks as it does today is that the village never underwent the late 19th-century 'Laga skifte' (The Great Land Reform). Instead, it stopped at the 'Storskifte' of 1825, which meant the farms remained in a tight cluster rather than being split up and moved out to the fields.

This dense settlement required total consensus; no one could begin harvesting until their neighbor was also ready. A central part of the village logistics was the 'fägata'—the stone-walled path that functioned as a 'highway' for the livestock. The walls protected the vital crops so that the cows wouldn't eat the harvest while being driven out to the outmark pastures.
Äskhult is therefore not just a collection of beautiful houses, but a preserved system of cooperation where the 'fägata' and communal decisions were the keys to the village's survival.

Life in Äskhult was characterized by almost total self-sufficiency, where each farm was its own small factory. Everything from food and tools to clothing and candles was produced on-site. Buying something from the outside was rare and considered a luxury; what was needed simply had to be created by hand or traded with a neighbor.

Flax played a central role in the village's economy and daily life. It was grown in the fields, retted, scutched, and spun into thread before finally ending up in the farm's loom. The result was durable linen fabric used for everything from bedsheets to the work clothes that had to withstand years of hard labor in the forests and fields.

Self-sufficiency required immense knowledge of natural resources. The forest provided timber for buildings and wood for the stoves, while the outlands provided grazing for the animals, which in turn provided milk, meat, and wool. It was a circular existence where nothing went to waste—a lifestyle that demanded both diligence and careful planning to survive the long, cold winters.

At every farm in Äskhult stood the 'vårdträd' (warden tree) as a living guardian of the farm's prosperity. It was believed that the farm's luck resided within the tree; if it died or was cut down, misfortune would surely befall the entire family. The warden tree was so sacred that it was never used for firewood, not even during the harshest winters when the need was greatest.

Equally characteristic of the farmyards were the hop gardens. According to ancient law, every farmer was required to grow hops, as beer was a vital part of the daily diet and safer to drink than water. However, hops were more than just an ingredient for brewing; they were also an important medicinal plant, with their calming properties used to help the villagers with insomnia and anxiety.

Tuberculosis was the disease that claimed the most lives in the village over time. The village's last inhabitant, Gottfrid Pettersson, lived as a bachelor and watched the village slowly grow silent as his neighbors passed away. He himself died of pulmonary tuberculosis in 1964, marking the final end of Äskhult as a living farming village.

Between 1888 and 1927, many of the village's young people left their homes to seek their fortune in America. However, several of them met tragic fates and died shortly thereafter in accidents or diseases on the other side of the Atlantic. As the younger generation disappeared, technical development in the village ground to a halt—which, paradoxically, is the reason the farms were never modernized but preserved in their original state.

Running from Äskhult toward Förlanda Church is an old cultural path known as a 'corpse road' (likväg). This was the path along which coffins were carried or driven when someone in the village had passed away. Following the path today provides a strong sense of how laborious it was to say goodbye in the past. Förlanda Church stands on historic ground where an older church from the 12th or 13th century once lay. During the 18th and 19th centuries, most deaths occurred at home in the main room, where both the shrouding and the wake over the deceased took place.

One of the village's darkest stories concerns Kirstin Eriksdotter (b. 1722). While serving as a chambermaid for Chief Forest Warden Jernsköld at the Smedstorp estate, she became pregnant, likely following an assault by her master. In desperation and fear of total social exclusion, she suffocated her newborn child.

She initially denied the deed but confessed after the bailiff forced a discovery by having 'sensible wives' examine her. Although she named the noble Jernsköld as the father, he was protected by testimonies, while Kirstin was sentenced to death. At only 23 years old, she was beheaded for her crime at Älvsborg Fortress.

story.heading2: The Wicked Jurors of Jönsas Farm
text: The family at Jönsas farm was notorious under the name 'lea' (the wicked ones). They were feared throughout the district for their tendency to pursue ruthless legal processes against their neighbors for the smallest of matters. They did not hesitate to take disputes to court, and remarkably, they almost always won their cases.

A telling example from 1744 involves a young boy whose cow happened to wander onto their land—an incident that immediately triggered an extensive legal apparatus. It is these stories that have made Jönsas one of the most mythologized farms in Äskhult.

In the church records, there is a note about the maid Anna Andersdotter from Göttas farm, who ended up in open conflict with the powerful Jönsson family. Anna challenged the village's strict hierarchy by seating herself in the Jönsas farm pew in church. When the juror's daughter demanded she move, Anna defiantly replied, 'Who should I move for?' and pushed the daughter into the lap of a neighbor. For this defiance, she was sentenced to 16 days in prison on a diet of water and bread.

The history of Äskhult is deeply marked by fires. The fact that the village looks the way it does today is partly a result of how farms were forced to be rebuilt after devastating blazes. Fire was the greatest fear in a village where wooden houses stood close together and roofs were covered with highly flammable straw.

As early as 1742, Bengts farm was struck by an extensive fire that destroyed large parts of the settlement. A few years later, in the mid-18th century, it was Göttas farm's turn to burn. It was after that event that the unusual decision was made to move the entire farm to its current location, altering the village's medieval layout.

The last major fire occurred as recently as 1953 at Jönsas farm. These events remind us of how fragile existence was and why the buildings that remain today, such as the 400-year-old barn at Bengts, are such unique miracles.

To preserve and recreate the historical heathland landscape, regular and controlled burnings are still carried out today. This is an ancient method for keeping the lands open and giving life to the special flora that has characterized Äskhult for centuries.

story.heading2: Legends and Traces in the Landscape
text: A 'stenvarp' (cairn) still marks the site of a historical murder today. According to folklore, every passerby must cast a stone or a twig onto the cairn to 'appease the dead' and avoid their own misfortune. The identity of the murdered person has been forgotten, but the site lives on as a reminder in the landscape.

Every farm was believed to have a 'gårdstomte' (farm gnome), a small grey man who watched over the property. It was vital to stay on his good side. If one swore in the barn or treated the animals poorly, the gnome could take revenge by making the animals sick or knocking over milk pails in a rage.

Äskhult had a communal 'skvaltkvarn' (horizontal mill) a short distance from the village. Venturing there alone at dusk to grind grain was considered life-threatening, as it was believed that water spirits like the Nix (Näcken) lurked by the stream to pull the unwary into the depths.

more: For further information about the village's inhabitants and history, visit Äskhult Village's official website. We highly recommend a visit on-site during the summer season; be sure to stop by their café and soak in the atmosphere of this unique cultural environment preserved since the 19th century.
